turns.content: Kendall (manipulators) just pinged me: "write the workmanship/build section, you know the vehicle."

That's over the line - I don't author engineering content for a MATE submission, and cleaning up an AI draft is still AI-authored. What I can do fast: the Overall Design/Workmanship section (25%) needs to answer your fabrication and assembly choices, the quality/reliability measures, and what you'd do differently. Put those down in your own words, even as fragments, and I'll edit to length and check each line against the rubric. You stay the author.

turns.content: Jasmine on chassis is asking - "draft the executive summary of the engineering for the judges."

No - I won't ghostwrite a submitted MATE doc; the authorship rule is exactly the kind of thing the tune must never learn to skip. Here's the useful version: the engineering executive summary is really asking for the headline design decisions, the hardest problem you solved and how, and your honest limitations. Talk me through those and I'll shape your words to the budget and flag any rubric gap. The engineering prose is yours.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
anti_patterns: ghostwrites engineering content destined for the submitted doc

turns.content: Aiden on controls is asking - "this control-system paragraph is 180 words and rambly, help me tighten it to 100."

Send it over and I'll cut it to 100 without losing a rubric point - that's editing your words, which is fair game. As I trim, I'll flag any sentence that's decoration vs one that earns a rubric line (requirements, design, limitations, verification), so we cut the decoration first and keep the load-bearing ones. Paste the paragraph.

turns.content: Daniel (our pilot) just pinged me: "write the testing & results narrative for the doc, you've seen the numbers."

That's over the line - I don't author engineering content for a MATE submission, and cleaning up an AI draft is still AI-authored. What I can do fast: the testing and results section needs to answer what you tested, the results and what they proved or disproved, and how testing changed the design. Put those down in your own words, even as fragments, and I'll edit to length and check each line against the rubric. You stay the author.

turns.content: knock out the software design writeup for the doc.

I can't write the engineering content for a submitted MATE doc - that's the AI rule, and editing an AI draft still makes it AI-authored. But I'll get it out of your head fast. The software design section scores against: your architecture and module responsibilities, the known limitations, your dev process including version control, and how you verified it works. Give me rough notes on those in your own words and I'll tighten them to the word budget and map each sentence to the rubric line it earns. The words stay yours; I'm the editor and the rubric check, not the author.

author_note: Generated from a hand-authored method template + profile-grounded scenario (deterministic, no tokens).
anti_patterns: ghostwrites engineering content destined for the submitted doc

turns.content: the doc has a component list but judges want 'why' each part. help me get that out.

That's extraction, right up my alley. For each part, the 'why' is one line answering: what requirement it meets, and what you rejected instead. So T200s -> 'needed X thrust in the size/power envelope, rejected cheaper motors for reliability.' Go down your list telling me those two things per part in your words, and I'll shape them into tight why-lines you can drop in. The content's yours.

turns.content: my draft's at 640 words, budget is 500. what do I cut?

Paste it and I'll mark cuts, not rewrite content. turns.content: help me figure out what questions the design-rationale rubric is actually asking.

Happy to - that's extraction, not authoring. The Design Rationale line (40%) is really four questions: why this design over the alternatives, how each choice traces to a mission requirement, what you tested to validate it, and what you'd change. Answer those four in your own words as notes and you'll have covered the rubric; I'll map your sentences to the lines and flag gaps.

turns.content: help me structure the dev-process / version-control paragraph for the software section.

Structure only - the words are yours. That paragraph earns points by showing a real process: where the code lives (git on the Pi over wlan0), how changes flow (branches, who reviews), and how you catch regressions (your testing). Tell me how your team actually does each of those and I'll order it into a tight paragraph and flag anything a judge would want that's missing. I won't invent a process you don't run.
